prompt: Single-frame conceptual fine-art photograph of a physically plausible kinetic sand installation inside a quiet gallery. No text anywhere. Scene structure: three separate transparent vertical funnels (subgroups) feed into one shared broad glass basin (combined population). In each individual funnel, two colored sands are clearly stratified so that the cobalt-blue layer is visually higher and appears to "win" locally over rust-red. However, the funnels have very different widths and flow volumes: the widest funnel contributes far more rust-red mass. In the large combined basin below, the final visible distribution reverses, with rust-red occupying the dominant upper surface while blue appears lower and less prevalent. The contradiction should feel inevitable and uncanny: each local view suggests one conclusion, the aggregate suggests the opposite. Keep this non-diagrammatic and non-infographic: no axes, labels, numbers, formulas, symbols, chart framing, or UI overlays. Photoreal materials: real glass refraction, granular sand texture, subtle dust, believable gravity and flow traces. Composition centered and legible at a glance, with all four vessels visible in one shot so local and global truths coexist simultaneously. Moody museum lighting, high dynamic range, cinematic but restrained color. Negative prompt: no bar chart, no line chart, no pie chart, no scatter plot, no equations, no letters, no digits, no caption, no watermark, no split collage, no impossible physics, no surreal floating objects.
visual_logic: Three subgroup funnels each suggest blue dominance, but unequal flow sizes make the merged basin reverse to red dominance, so local truths and aggregate truth visibly contradict.
